coredata.dc:title: ONE STEP GREEN SYNTHESIS OF CARBON QUANTUM DOTS AND ITS APPLICATION TOWARDS THE BIOELECTROANALYTICAL AND BIOLABELING STUDIES
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Shereema, Rayammarakkar M.
Sankar, Vandana
Raghu, K.G
Rao, Talasila P.
Shankar, S.Sharath
coredata.dc:description: A green luminescent carbon quantum dots were prepared from maltose by Microwave assisted method followed by passivation with NaOH (pH=7.4). The TEM measurement confirmed the average size of prepared carbon quantum dots to be 2nm. Surface characterization such as XPS, FTIR and Raman spectroscopy confirm that the functional groups (C=O, C-OH) were attached on the surface of sp2 hybridized carbon. Electrochemical characterization studies on carbon paste electrode (CPE) revealed that the synthesized carbon quantum dots showed higher electrocatalytic property, conductivity and surface area. Therefore, herein we report the synthesized carbon quantum dots could find its applicability as an electrochemical sensor for the detection of neurotransmitter, dopamine. Cyclic voltammetry and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was employed for the detection of DA in presence of common interferences like UA, and AA with carbon quantum dots modified carbon paste electrode. The developed sensor was effectively applied for the real sample analysis with satisfactory results. Moreover, biological studies in He La cell lines proclaimed that the cell viability was unaffected (100% viability) on incubation with the carbon dots. Significant cellular uptake as revealed by fluorescence imaging makes them suitable for cell labeling studies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Carbon paste electrode
Cyclic voltammetry
Fluorescence. Real sample analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical behaviour of DA on CQDs/CPE
Effect of solution pH on the oxidation behaviour of DA
Analytical performance of CQDs/CPE towards DA in presence of AA and UA as interferents
Application of CQDs/CPE towards real sample analysis
Application of CQDs towards the cell labelling

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of CQDs/CPE and the mechanism of electrochemical oxidation/reduction of DA on CQDs/CPE.
(a) UV–vis spectra and inset shows appearance of CQDs in presence and absence of UV light (b) PL spectra of the CQDs at different wavelength.
(a) DLS (b) TEM image (c) particle size distribution of CQDs.
(a) XPS wide spectrum of CQDs (b) XPS of O 1s (c) C 1s spectrum of CQDs.
(a) Raman (b) FTIR spectra of the CQDs.
(i) Cyclic voltammograms of CPE (curve a), CQDs/CPE (curve b) in 1mM K3Fe(CN)6 and 0.1M KCl (ii) in 0.1M PBS containing 10−6 M dopamine.
(a) Chronoamperometric determination of DA with different concentration with a time interval of 30seconds (b) Plot of concentration of DA Vs Current.
Cyclic voltammograms of 1mM AA, 1.0μM DA and 0.1mM UA at CPE (curve a) and on CQDs/CPE (curve b) in pH 7.4 PBS. Scan rate: 50mV/s. DPV of same analytes on CQDs/CPE (inset).
(a) Transmitted light image (b) fluorescent image of HeLa cell after incubation with green GQDs for 24h.

head.ce:title: ONE STEP GREEN SYNTHESIS OF CARBON QUANTUM DOTS AND ITS APPLICATION TOWARDS THE BIOELECTROANALYTICAL AND BIOLABELING STUDIES

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Sciences & Technology Division (CSTD), Trivandrum 695019, INDIA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Agroprocessing and Natural Products Division (ANPD), CSIR-National Institute for Interdisciplinary Science & Technology (CSIR-NIIST), Trivandrum 695019, INDIA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CSIR-National Institute for Interdisciplinary Science & Technology (CSIR-NIIST)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR), New Delhi 110001, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A green luminescent carbon quantum dots were prepared from maltose by Microwave assisted method followed by passivation with NaOH (pH=7.4). The TEM measurement confirmed the average size of prepared carbon quantum dots to be 2nm. Surface characterization such as XPS, FTIR and Raman spectroscopy confirm that the functional groups (C=O, C-OH) were attached on the surface of sp2 hybridized carbon. Electrochemical characterization studies on carbon paste electrode (CPE) revealed that the synthesized carbon quantum dots showed higher electrocatalytic property, conductivity and surface area. Therefore, herein we report the synthesized carbon quantum dots could find its applicability as an electrochemical sensor for the detection of neurotransmitter, dopamine. Cyclic voltammetry and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was employed for the detection of DA in presence of common interferences like UA, and AA with carbon quantum dots modified carbon paste electrode. The developed sensor was effectively applied for the real sample analysis with satisfactory results. Moreover, biological studies in He La cell lines proclaimed that the cell viability was unaffected (100% viability) on incubation with the carbon dots. Significant cellular uptake as revealed by fluorescence imaging makes them suitable for cell labeling studies.

ce:section.ce:para: In the year 2004, during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes by preparative electrophoresis, a new type of carbon material with size below 10nm was obtained. This newly obtained carbon material exhibited high fluorescence and were highly soluble in water. It is now well recognized as carbon quantum dots and is an emerging star in the world of nano materials [1]. Till date there are so many methods adopted for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots including arc discharge [1], electrochemical synthesis [2,3], combustion/thermal/hydrothermal/acidic oxidation [4–7], cage-opening of fullerene [8], supported synthesis [9–11], microwave/ultrasonic [12,13], laser ablation [14,15] and electrochemical oxidation methods [16,17] and so on. Carbon quantum dots gained greater acceptance because of their wide application for instance in bioimaging [18,19], nanomedicine [20,21], photocatalysis [22,23], electrocatalysis [24,25], biosensing [26,27], and chemical sensing [28,29]. It was also used as Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Pb2+and Ag+ sensors [30–34]. These dots were also reported with its application as pH sensors by Jia et al. [35]. Li et al. stated that the Nitrogen-Doped Graphene Quantum Dots supported by graphene sheets have been found to possess superior electrocatalytic ability [36]. Recently in 2015 Xu et al. reported Sulfur-doped graphitic carbon nitride decorated with graphene quantum dots which could be used as an efficient metal-free electrocatalyst [37].

Dopamine (DA) is an important biomolecule belongs to the catecholamine family. Being monoamine neurotransmitter, that plays a critical role in the function of the central nervous, hormonal, and cardiovascular systems [38,39]. Since its discovery, DA has attracted a great deal of attention in clinical fields because its abnormal levels indicate various diseases, such as Parkinson's disease, senile dementia, and schizophrenia [40]. Over the past several decades, tremendous effort has been made and various techniques have been developed, such as fluorimetry, chemiluminescence, capillary electrophoresis, and ion chromatography [41–44]. Being electrochemically active electrochemical detection of DA could be possible. This approach got much more attention due to its fast detection, simplicity, reproducibility, impressive cost-effectiveness, and potential for miniaturization [45]. However, the electrochemical response to DA can easily be affected by ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), and other molecule due to the closeness of their oxidation potential. Various approaches have been made for the detection of DA individually and simultaneously. Unfortunately most of the fabricated electrode failed to detect them in the nano molar level.

In this work we are proposing a one-step green synthesis of green luminescent carbon quantum dots from maltose, which was further used for cell labeling studies. Electrochemical characterization studies using carbon paste electrode revealed that carbon dots provided higher surface area, conductivity and electrocatalytic activity. By taking advantage of this characteristic, CQDs/CPE was successfully applied for the determination of nano molar level of dopamine individually and in the presence of ascorbic acid and uric acid by cyclic voltammetry, further it was confirmed by differential pulse voltammetry. To our knowledge, this is the first report on the application of carbon dots/CPE for electrochemical sensing.

Maltose was purchased from Merck, Mumbai, India. Dopamine hydrochloride and uric acid were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Milwauke, WI, USA, ascorbic acid was purchased from Riedel-de haen chemicals. DA stock solution was prepared in 0.1M perchloric acid; whereas UA and AA in double distilled water. Afore mentioned chemicals were of analytical grade and were used without any further purification. Standard method was employed in the preparation of phosphate buffer solution (PBS). The water used was double distilled. All the experiments were performed at room temperature.

The size and morphology was studied by a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) on a FEI, TECNAI 30G2 S-TWIN microscope with an accelerating voltage of 300kV. XPS studies were carried out by x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (model ESCALAB220IXL) equipped with an Axis Ultra, Kratos (1486.7eV) monochromatic source for excitation. XPS spectra over a binding energy (BE) range of 0–1200eV were obtained using analyzer pass energy of 117.4eV. UV- Visible spectra were recorded with a Shimadzu computer controlled double beam UV–vis spectrophotometer UV-2401PC (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan), The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of the C-dot was recorded on a Spex-Fluorolog FL22 spectrofuorimeter equipped with a double grating 0.22 m Spex 1680 monochromator and a 450W Xe lamp as the excitation source. The IR spectra were recorded as KBr pellets on a Perkin-Elmer Spectrum One FT-IR spectrometer operating between 4500 and 400cm−1. Raman spectra were recorded with a confocal Raman microscope WITec alpha 300R (WITec GmbH, Ulm, Germany). A laser beam with 633nm from air cooled argon was used for excitation. The Electrochemical measurements were performed using a three-electrode cell (20ml) fitted with a CQDs/CPE or CPE (3mm in diameter) as working electrode, Pt wire as auxiliary electrode, and a potassium chloride (KCl) saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as reference electrode using a VSP-potentiostat/galvanostat (Biologic Science Instruments).

Maltose was digested in a microwave oven (Panasonic Model NN-GT231M) for 20minutes. The charring product was stirred for half an hour at 800rpm with a much diluted solution of NaOH (100ml of 8×10−5 M) of pH 7.4 as passivizing agent. During this process more and more –OH groups will attach on the sp2 hybridized carbon surface, which will lead to the enrichment of quantum yield of carbon quantum dots. The unreacted and other impurities were eliminated by centrifugation. The resultant solution was then sonicated and filtered with Whatmann filter paper. The obtained carbon quantum dots were stored at room temperature. The proposed method was repeatable and reproducible.

CQDs/CPE was prepared by manually grinding 70% graphite powder and 30% silicon oil in an agitate mortar for about 30minute to get a homogenous mixture. The paste was packed into the cavity of CPE and smoothened on weighing paper. 15μL CQD was drop casted on the surface of carbon paste electrode for 15min. After this, the electrode was washed with water and data were recorded in pH 7.4 PBS.

Fig. 1 a (inset) showed the physical characteristics of prepared CQDs under visible and UV light. UV Visible absorption spectroscopy (Fig. 1a) gave two characteristic peaks, one at 220nm corresponding to π− π∗ transition of the conjugated C=C band and another at 280nm corresponding to n− π∗ transition of C=O band. The PL spectrum (Fig. 1(b)) revealed that maximum emission intensity was observed at 436nm when excited at 360nm. In addition to that, a regular bathochromic shift (up to 600nm) in the emission maxima was observed when the excitation window was changed from 360 to 480nm. Tang et al. suggested that this kind of bathochromic shift is common a characteristic of carbon-based QDs [46]. Quantum yields (QY) of the C-dots were calculated using the equation
Φ s
=
Φ R
A R
n
2
s
I S
/
A S
n
2
R
I R
Where, ФS and ФR are quantum yields of sample and reference respectively; AR and AS are absorbance of the reference and sample at excitation wavelengths; nS and nR are refractive indices of the sample medium and reference medium respectively; IS and IR are integrated fluorescence intensities of sample and reference respectively and was found to be 8% which is comparable with the reported data [46]. (Quinine sulfate (Ф=0.54) was used as reference to determine QYs of C-dots).

The size of the CQDs was confirmed to be 2.49nm from DLS (Fig. 2 a). In addition, TEM analysis (Fig. 2b) revealed that highly monodispersed and spherical C-dots with average size 2nm were obtained, which was further summarized in PSD (Fig. 2c). C-dots were subjected to XPS in order to obtain information about the functional group present on them. XPS spectrum (Fig. 3 a) showed a peak at 284.8eV and another at 532eV corresponding to graphitic C1s and O1s respectively. The deconvolution of the dominated C1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) showed three peaks corresponding to CC (284.5eV), C-O (286.5eV) and CO (288.6eV) respectively.

Fig. 4 a displayed Raman spectrum of carbon dots. A major peak at 1578cm−1 and two minor peaks at 1331cm−1 and 2654cm−1 corresponding to G band and 2D bands respectively which confirmed the sp2 nature of carbon quantum dots. In order to get structural insight, FT-IR spectrum of CQDs was recorded. A peak corresponding to CO group at 1691cm−1 and another peak corresponding to aromatic CC (ring stretching) at 1625cm−1 was depicted clearly by Fig. 4b. Similarly, a broad peak at 3340cm−1 could be assigned to –OH stretching frequency. The peaks due to C–O stretching and bending vibrations appeared in the range of 1031 and 1143cm−1, respectively. A peak corresponding to COH emerged in the range 1353cm−1.

Electrochemical experiments were performed to examine the electrocatalytic activity of CQDs/CPE. Fig. 5 (i) showed the cyclic voltammogram of bare CPE (curve a) and CQDs/CPE (curve b) in 1mM K3[Fe(CN)6] with 0.1M KCl as the supporting electrolyte. The CQDs/CPE in K3 [Fe (CN)6] electrochemical redox probe showed an anodic peak potential (Epa) at 0.210V and a cathodic peak potential (Epc) at 0.147V. ΔEp (Epa-Epc) was 60mV, which showed the reversibility of the modified electrode. Comparing both the anodic (23.27μA) and cathodic peak current (−32.18μA) at bare CPE, a drastic change in anodic (208.80μA) and cathodic peak current (−240.89μA) was observed at CQDs/CPE which approved the fact that CQDs lifted the conductivity of bare CPE.

In order to compare the surface area of bare CPE and CQDs/CPE cyclic voltammogram of K3[Fe (CN)6] in 0.1M KCl with varying scan rate from 50 to 150mV/s were plotted (Fig. S1a & S2a). As per the Randles–Sevcik equation,
I p
= 2.69 ×
10
5
n
3 / 2
A C
0
D R
1 / 2
υ
1 / 2
Where, Ip is the peak current, n is the number of electrons transferred, A is the surface area of the electrode (cm2), DR is the diffusion coefficient of the molecule in solution (cm2/s), ʋ is the scan rate (V/s) and Co corresponds to the bulk concentration of the probe (mol/dm3). The surface area could be calculated from the slope of the plot of Ip Vs ʋ1/2 (1mM K3[Fe(CN)6], n =1, DR =7.6×10−6 cms−1) and was found to be 0.0429cm2 (from Fig. S1b) for CQDs/CPE and 0.00817 cm2 (from Fig. S2b) for CPE. All this results approved that CQDs provide larger surface area and high conductivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behaviour of DA on CQDs/CPE
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared CQDs/CPE was employed for the analysis of 1μM of DA in 0.1M PBS of pH 7.4. The poor sensitivity of bare CPE towards the redox reaction of DA was improved with CQDs/MCPE, Fig. 5(ii). DA undergoes oxidation at a potential of 0.113V with peak current 2.26μA and reduction at a potential 0.022V with peak current 0.37μA on the surface of bare CPE (curve a). On the other hand oxidation (0.102V) and reduction peak potentials (0.06V) of DA on CQDs/CPE (curve b) were shifted to less positive sides. In spite of this, a strong increase in oxidation (51.5μA) and reduction peak current (29.15μA) was also observed. This suggested the higher sensitivity and catalytic activity of CQDs/CPE towards the electrochemical oxidation of DA.

The effect of scan rate (ν) on the oxidation of DA on CQDs/CPE and CPE in 0.1M PBS of pH 7.4 were studied by cyclic voltammetry (Fig. S3a & S4a). While increase in the scan rate from 70-150mV/s both the oxidation and reduction peaks of DA increased with a small shift in potential.

From the equation
E p
= ( 2.303 R T / n α F ) log ν + c o n s tan t
where Ep is potential, R is gas constant, T is the temperature, n is number of electron transferred and F is the Faraday constant, electron transfer coefficient (α) was calculated from the slope of the plot of Ep Vs log ʋ and was found to be 0.69 for CQDs/CPE and 0.59 for CPE. From the same equation, the electron transfer constant was obtained as 0.018 for the modified electrode and 0.002 for that of the bare electrode (Fig. S3b & S4b).

On the basis of the slopes of the linear dependence of the anodic peak currents on the square root of the potential sweep rates, by the Randel–Sevcik equation,
I p
= ( 2.99 ×
10
5
)
α
1 / 2
n
3 / 2
A C D
1 / 2
ν
1 / 2
Where Ip is the peak current, A is the electrode surface area, D is the diffusion co-efficient and C is the bulk concentration, the diffusion coefficient of dopamine was calculated to be 0.929cm2/s for CQDs/CPE and 0.096cm2/s for CPE (Fig. S3c & S4c).

Single step potential chronoamperometry was employed to investigate the electrochemical process at the bare and modified electrodes. Fig. S5a & S6a showed the current–time curves of CQDs/CPE and CPE in 0.1M PBS with and without dopamine by applying a potential of 1.2V. Rate constant was calculated from the plot IC/IL Vs t1/2 obtained from the equation
I C
/
I L
=
π
1 / 2
( k C t )
1 / 2
where IC is catalytic current and IL is limiting current, K is rate constant, C is concentration of the analyte in mol dm−3 and t is time and was found to be 2.796×108 and 2.408×107 cm3 mol−1 s−1for CQDs/CPE and CPE respectively (Fig. S5b & S6b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of solution pH on the oxidation behaviour of DA
ce:section.ce:para: pH studies revealed that the redox reaction of DA on the surface of CQDs/CPE was largely dependent on pH of the solution. The effect of pH on the electrode signal and oxidation potential of 1μM DA in solution was investigated using cyclic voltammetry. As shown in Fig. S7a, with increase in pH, the peak potential (Epa) for DA became more negative on CQDs/CPE. The plot of Epa versus pH was linear with a slope of 63mV/pH and the equation of best fit being: Epa=0.063 pH+0.54 (R2 =0.995). The involvement of equal number of protons and electrons in the electrochemical process could be inferred from this result. Moreover, the plot of Epa vs pH graph clearly indicated that there was a catalytic peak shift to a more negative potential with increasing pH. From Fig. S7b, a gradual increase in the peak current of DA with the increase of pH from 5.4 to 7.4 was observed with a maximum value at pH 7.4, which drastically decreased with further increase in pH.

Chronoamperometric experiments were used to evaluate the analytical performance of the modified electrode. Chronoamperometric responses for the increasing concentrations of dopamine added at regular intervals along the ranges of concentration from 0.01 to 500μM in 0.1M PBS at 1200mV was displayed in Fig. 6 a. The corresponding calibration graph of current vs. dopamine concentration portrayed linearity in the lower concentration region (0.01 to 1μM) with R2 =0.999 as well as in the higher concentration range (5 to 500μM) with R2 =0.999 (Fig. 6b). The detection limit for dopamine was determined from the calibration graph to be 0.004μM based on thrice the standard deviation of blank. The analytical performance of CQDs/CPE was compared with the reported electrodes and it is depicted in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of CQDs/CPE towards DA in presence of AA and UA as interferents
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs/CPE was introduced for the electrochemical analysis of ternary system consisting 1μМ DA, 1mM AA and 0.1mM UA in 0.1M PBS of pH 7.4 with scan rate of 50mV/s (Fig. 7 ). On the surface of bare CPE (curve a) all the three analytes exhibited an overlapped voltammogram at a potential of 0.125V. This was further converted to three well separated oxidation peaks on CQDs/CPE (curve b). On CQDs/CPE, AA, DA and UA undergo oxidation at −0.112V, 0.106V and 0.180V respectively which was further confirmed by Differntial Pulse Voltammetry (inset). The peak to peak separation for DA-AA and UA-DA were 0.218V and 0.08V respectively, which were large enough to identify them simultaneously. Interference studies using DPV reveal that the increase in concentration of DA was not interfering with the oxidation of other analytes (Fig. S8). Even at higher concentration of AA and UA oxidation peak of DA did not alter. This was strong evidence to the capability of the fabricated electrode towards the electrochemical analysis of DA in presence of AA and UA.

The prepared CQDs modified CPE poses a negatively charged surface due to the hydroxyl groups present on the carbon quantum dots. Hence the positively charged dopamine will interact with the negative layer on the electrode surface (Scheme 1 ). This electrostatic interaction will be the driving force for the rapid oxidation and reduction processes of dopamine in 0.1M PBS of pH of 7.4.

The relative standard deviation of 5 successive measurements for DA level of 1μM was 2.0%. When not in use, the CQDs/CPE was stored in air at room temperature. After one week of storage at room temperature, 95% of the initial response was observed, indicating the stability and reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CQDs/CPE towards real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of CQDs/CPE was tested by introducing it for the detection of dopamine in blood serum sample, which revealed acceptable results (Table S2). Serum samples were obtained from Amrita Institute of Medical Science and Research centre, Cochin, Kerala, India. 5mL of blood sample was centrifuged (1000rpm for 20minutes) to remove plasma. Appropriate aliquot (200μL) of clear serum samples was used for the recovery studies of DA. Recovery studies were performed by spiking known amounts of standard solutions of DA to the diluted (100 times) serum samples followed by the analysis using the modified electrode under optimized analytical conditions. The appreciable recovery and R.S.D. suggested that the proposed methods could be efficiently used for the determination of DA in serum with recovery in the range 99.8–101.1%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CQDs towards the cell labelling
ce:section.ce:para: Biological studies were carried out in He La cell line model to evaluate cellular uptake and cytotoxicity, on incubation with the CQDs. The CQDs with no functionalization were found to enter the cells, evident from the localization of green fluorescence inside the cells (Fig. 8 ). Cell viability was found to be unaffected after incubation of the cells with CQDs for a 24h time period. Detailed protocols have been given in supporting information.

A green luminescent carbon quantum dot of 2nm diameter was prepared from maltose. Electrochemical studies revealed that the CQDs show high electro catalytic activity, high conductivity and provide large surface area. CQDs/CPE, a new type of electrochemical sensor could detect nanomolar levels of dopamine. It is to be noted that the CQDs/CPE could be used for the simultaneous analysis of dopamine, ascorbic acid and uric acid. The developed CQDs/CPE was successfully applied to real sample analysis. Being non cytotoxic, the fluorescence property of the carbon quantum dots is useful for cell imaging application. These CQDs enter into cell without any further functionalization and fluorescence property of the particle could be used to track their position in cell using conventional fluorescence microscope. The fabricated CQDs/CPE and its application will unambiguously lead to more research in the field of biosensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Ms Shereema R. M. and Sharath shankar are thankful to CSIR, New Delhi, India for the award of Senior Research Fellowship and Nehru Postdoctoral Fellowship respectively. Ms. Vandana Sankar acknowledges Department of Biotechnology for the award of Postdoctoral Fellowship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2015.09.145.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
One-pot synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoribbons, nanoparticles, and graphene by the exfoliation of graphite in ionic liquids
Facile preparation of low cytotoxicity fluorescent carbon nanocrystals by electrooxidation of graphite
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles derived from candle soot
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles: synthesis, characterization, and bioimaging application
Nanosized carbon particles from natural gas soot
Transforming C60 molecules into graphene quantum dots
An aqueous route to multicolor photoluminescent carbon dots using silica spheres as carriers
Synthesis of photoluminescent carbogenic dots using mesoporous silica spheres as nanoreactors
Microwave assisted one-step green synthesis of cell-permeable multicolor photoluminescent carbon dots without surface passivation reagents
One-step synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by laser irradiation
An electrochemical avenue to blue luminescent nanocrystals from multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs)
Large scale electrochemical synthesis of high quality carbon nanodots and their photocatalytic property
Extremely high inhibition activity of photoluminescent carbon nanodots toward cancer cells
Commercially activated carbon as the source for producing multicolor photoluminescent carbon dots by chemical oxidation
Nanoparticles as vehicles for delivery of photodynamic therapy agents
In vivo biodistribution and toxicology of functionalized nano-graphene oxide in mice after oral and intraperitoneal administration
Near-infrared light controlled photocatalytic activity of carbon quantum dots for highly selective oxidation reaction
Metal nanoparticle/carbon quantum dot composite as a photocatalyst for high- efficiency cyclohexane oxidation
Metal organic frameworks for electrochemical applications
Graphene-Based Carbon Nitride Nanosheets as Efficient Metal-Free Electrocatalysts for Oxygen Reduction Reactions
Amorphous carbon nanoparticles: a versatile label for rapid diagnostic (immuno) assays
Analytical sensitivity limits for lateral flow immunoassays
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots-based fluorescent chemosensors for sensitive and selective detection of mercury ions and application of imaging in living cells

Facile bulk production of highly blue fluorescent graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots and their application as highly selective and sensitive sensors for the detection of mercuric and iodide ions in aqueous media
One-pot green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticles as fluorescent probes for mercury ions
Highly selective and sensitive detection of Cu2+ with lysine enhancing bovine serum albumin modified-carbon dots fluorescent probe
Graphitic carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing platform for highly efficient detection of Fe3+ ions
Synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots via simple acid hydrolysis of bovine serum albumin and its potential as sensitive sensing probe for lead (II) ions
Highly luminescent n-doped carbon quantum dots as an effective multifunctional fluorescence sensing platform
One-pot green synthesis of optically pH-sensitive carbon dots with upconversion luminescence
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots with oxygen-rich functional groups
Sulfur-doped graphitic carbon nitride Decorated with graphene quantum dots for an efficient metal-free electrocatalyst
Nur77 and retinoid X receptors: crucial factors in dopamine-related neuroadaptation
The highly sensitive detection of catecholamines using a microfluidic device integrated with an enzyme-modified pre-reactor for interferent elimination and an interdigitated array electrode
Association studies of the adenosine A2a receptor (1976T>C) genetic polymorphism in Parkinson's disease and schizophrenia
Aromatic glycinonitriles and methylamines as pre-column fluorescence derivatization reagents for catecholamines
Trivalent copper chelate-luminol chemiluminescence system for highly sensitive CE detection of dopamine in biological sample after clean- up using SPE
Determination of monoamine transmitters and their metabolites by capillary electrophoresis with electrochemical detection
Determination of dopamine and epinephrine by ion chromatography with fluorescence detector
Sensitive and selective electrochemical detection of dopamine using an electrode modified with carboxylated carbonaceous spheres
Deep ultraviolet photoluminescence of water-soluble self- passivated graphene quantum dots
Multi-walled carbon nanotube/poly(glycine) modified carbon paste electrode for the determination of dopamine in biological fluids and pharmaceuticals
Simultaneous determination of dopamine in presence of ascorbic acid with fe-ag ctab nanoparticles carbon paste electrode
One-pot synthesis of graphene–chitosan nanocomposite modified carbon paste electrode for selective determination of dopamine
A dopamine sensor based on a carbon paste electrode modified with DNA-doped poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene)
Cyclic voltammetric investigations of dopamine at alizarin modified carbon paste electrode
